data.epi_content:
"Fuck all of you, you idiots. I just wish everything would collapse, you, you, you, I just wish you would all die..."

Squelch.

The sword fails to sever her neck in one stroke and gets stuck.

Though it must be painful, Erica merely blinks slightly without screaming.

It must hurt, so why is she wearing that expressionless face as if nothing's wrong?

Everyone who had been shouting for her execution suddenly falls silent.

Even though she's a heinous criminal, she's still just a young girl who's probably only about 17 years old, reduced to such a state.

The boy holding the sword wipes sweat from his forehead and raises the sword high to strike again.

With a face that doesn't match the scene at all, Erica's head—which had been spewing curses—flies through the air.

And then, I make eye contact with Erica in the sky.

If I'm not mistaken, she was definitely looking at me with a slightly relieved expression, the corners of her mouth turning up slightly.

They say the blood of executed criminals brings good fortune.

And the higher the criminal's status, the more fortune it brings.

The fact that she was just a young girl who had been breathing moments ago, who didn't die from a single sword stroke but suffered a painful death, didn't seem to matter much.

After all, who would refuse the chance for good fortune just by being sprinkled with a dead person's blood?

So the gathered crowd rushes forward as one, trying to catch the blood spurting from Erica's neck with their bodies.

Most stretch out their hands to catch Erica's head as it flies through the air.

Even Evan and I reach out as well.

Erica's head doesn't fall where Evan and I reach.

People who never even spoke to her, who aren't even her blood relatives, catch her head, pull at her hair, and try to snatch it away from each other.

"E-Evan. Shouldn't we do something...?"

"A...ah...uh...ah...uh. Erica. Ah...aah."

Evan was in shock.

He's no help at all.

And he calls himself Erica's childhood friend.

If someone had asked Erica who she loved most in the world, she would have named herself first, and then Evan.

What on earth is this guy doing?

If it had been someone else's head, I probably would have felt slightly depressed about how people could do such horrible things so casually, but I would have walked away without doing anything.

Maybe Erica thought we were friends, but it was just me who thought that.

But seeing such a scene makes me angry.

It's Erica after all. Maybe it doesn't mean much to me, but still.

Wind gathers, and flames hot enough not to burn people to death fly toward the crowd.

It wasn't me who did it.

Anyway, they start to scatter, screaming.

"Why... why are you all doing such terrible things so casually?

She's dead now, so leave her alone. She must have had a hard enough time while alive. Why? Why? Ah."

Evan says this and then falls on his backside.

As if all his strength has suddenly left him.

In a faint whisper, I hear him add:

"I have no right to say that."

I pretend not to hear.

If Evan starts blaming himself, I'll end up blaming myself too.

At least this open plaza isn't the place to fall into self-loathing.

The skin was just slightly hot, but no one was hurt.

Of course I controlled the power.

He must be around 40.

The man with the scruffy beard holds Erica's head close to his chest like a precious treasure and shows no sign of letting go.

So I create several sharp icicles in the air and aim them at him.

"Put it down. There are plenty of other heads up there waiting to be cut off."

"T-this is mine! I picked it up!"

I spin one of the icicles and shoot it right next to the man's foot.

"The next one will be aimed at your arm... oh, he's gone."

Only then does the man carefully place Erica's head on the ground and flee into the crowd.
data.epi_content2:
I walk toward it.

I pick up Erica and look at her.

"Hey, Miss Erica."

My impression from when our eyes met in the air wasn't wrong.

Erica's pupils were slightly dilated, and the corners of her mouth were turned up.

As I take each step, people scatter like the parting of the sea, either out of fear or because they think I'm a noble.

Not particularly caring, I close Erica's open eyes.

Even with her eyes closed, the dark circles beneath them look heavy.

As if her skin is sagging.

"You hate me, don't you?

Since I've touched you with my bare hands, you can slap me if you want, so please get up."

There's no answer.

So I speak to Evan, who's still alive and therefore capable of answering.

"Hey Evan, Erica is dead."

"......"

But was I mistaken?

Evan doesn't answer either.

Evan was complicated in his own way.

The last words he exchanged with Erica were meant only to hurt each other, digging into each other's hearts.

That's why he couldn't stand up for Erica.

If he had left her alone, those vile people would have surely torn away her eyes, skin, and hair.

He constrained himself with strange thoughts about whether he had the right.

Perhaps at that moment, Erica was begging to be saved.

When news came that her family had fallen, no, when her brother was branded a traitor and beheaded, he should have gone to comfort her.

Instead, he used that fact to attack Erica.

He killed someone with his tongue.

She had no one to turn to at the Academy except for Evan himself.

She even slapped Vivian, hoping she wouldn't approach her.

He knew that Lydia was Erica's right-hand woman.

And he thought that if the family collapsed, Lydia might bully Erica.

The thought that crossed his mind then was the idiotic idea that 'if she gets bullied a bit, she might realize she did something wrong.'

"Ugh...urp!"

As soon as that thought crossed his mind, Evan began vomiting in the street.

He hadn't eaten anything, so only stomach acid came up, but all he could do was cry and think about how disgusting he was.

They receive Erica's body and leave.

And the place they leave behind begins to grow noisy again with cheers.

New prisoners were coming up to spray blood into the sky.

Unlike Erica, they didn't spew curses but begged for mercy, pleading for their lives as they lost them one by one.

Until the moment of death, they desperately clung to their precious lives, but Erica had let go of hers long before, even while she was still breathing normally.

That day, the day I went to see Erica.

Vivian knew there were two bullets in the gun.

...............

"I should have done something when she said it wasn't meant for me."

Saying it wasn't meant for me implied she was going to use it.

It meant she would either make an extreme choice like her father, or shoot other people.

How far can I get by saying I wasn't thinking straight?

"She said it was better to smile than to get angry.

When she said a stupid girl who had lost everything could do nothing but smile, I should have at least hugged her."

When she had such a pitiful, but numb and barren face, I should have approached her.

Even if she pushed me away, even if she hated it, somehow.
data.epi_content3:
"When she asked for the gun back, I should have either brought it from where I'd put it on the bed or just destroyed it."

If I had known this would happen—though I couldn't have known.

If only she had asked me to deal with that woman Lydia.

Even if I didn't kill her, I could have threatened her like I did with the man who was holding Erica just now.

"When she pointed the gun at her head and pulled the trigger, I should have used my magic to turn the gun to dust instead of just pouncing on her."

Come to think of it, I'm excellent at swordsmanship, and my magic is better than Evan's except in theory.

I wouldn't be overpowered by anyone else, but for this girl, all she had was a gun that fired metal pieces.

Maybe that's why this happened.

If she was so weak that she couldn't kill someone without a gun, then she must have endured such bullying, or rather suffered it.

Because she had no way to escape with her own power.

"When she asked me to jump off the terrace, I should have jumped while using flight magic."

If she could say such things so casually.

If she could pull a trigger that might blow her head off so casually.

Even if I'd heard she might bite her tongue and die, I should have stayed by her side somehow.

"She hates me.

She said what happened to her was the natural course of events, and it was all because of me."

I don't know why it's my fault, but if Erica said so, then it must be true.

I'll just think that way.

I didn't feel any of her characteristic clinging malice or gloominess when she said she hated me, but since Erica said so.

I have to live with guilt.

I shouldn't live proudly or confidently.

"Hey, Evan. What should I do?"

And so, after being shut in a dark room for a week, I asked a question to Evan, who was muttering incomprehensible words.

"Was it this face that was the problem?

Or was it a problem that you fell for me?

Was it a problem that I didn't want to grow distant from Erica?

Was it wrong that I confided in you and received comfort on the day I was slapped?

From the beginning, I never wanted something like love.

Everyone tells me they like me, they love me, they confess their feelings.

And then, like now, so many things break.

I lost my family, I never made a single friend in my neighborhood because of it, no one ever showed me pure goodwill.

Except for Erica when I first met her.

Even Erica later...

Anyway, even after entering the Academy, no one except Erica treated me with such purposeless goodwill and friendliness.

Everyone, everyone, like you, even His Highness the Crown Prince pretends not to be interested, like other men.

But everyone looks at me with those eyes.

Even the child sitting in the farthest seat who I've never exchanged a word with.

Honestly, I don't know.

Maybe when Erica slapped me and you comforted me, I felt a ticklish feeling.

But now I think it was all an illusion.

Useless, the kind that only makes everyone miserable."

That was a bit long.

"Hey, Evan. What was the problem?"

"That's... right. What was the, *cough*, problem."

Evan lies on the bed and gives his answer.

"I was the problem."

That's how Evan answered.

"Me too."

I respond to Evan's words.

And I didn't cry.

I don't show much emotion.

Just as Erica once did.

But I couldn't imitate her completely.

A tear trickled from my right eye, and then enough tears flowed to soak an entire handkerchief.

So I went to my room, filled the bathtub with warm water, and sobbed in there.

"How am I supposed to live like this?"
